content.text: AWS operates a suite of network and security services that run entirely at edge PoPs — the 600+ network facilities co-located in cities worldwide. These services are distinct from Region-based services: they execute at the point where user traffic
the AWS network, before any packet crosses a long-haul backbone link. The five core edge networking services are
(CDN + edge compute). Each operates at a different protocol layer and solves a different class of problem.

attrs.alt: AWS Edge PoP service layers: Shield Standard → Route 53 → WAF → Global Accelerator → CloudFront + Lambda@Edge/CF Functions → AWS origin

content.text: AWS Shield: DDoS at the Edge

is automatically active on every CloudFront distribution and Route 53 hosted zone at no charge. It inspects traffic at Layers 3 and 4 as it arrives at PoP network interfaces and drops attack packets before they consume PoP resources. Shield handles SYN flood, UDP reflection, ICMP flood, and other volumetric and protocol attacks.
L3/L4 volumetric and protocol attacks; automatic, always on
L3–L7 including sophisticated application-layer attacks; 24/7 DRT team; cost protection
Shield Advanced adds protection for EC2, ELB, and Global Accelerator in addition to CloudFront and Route 53. The
can write custom WAF rules during an active attack and AWS provides cost protection so you are not billed for extra data transfer charges incurred during a DDoS event.

# Enable Shield Advanced on a resource
aws shield create-protection \
  --name 'my-cloudfront-protection' \
  --resource-arn arn:aws:cloudfront::123456789012:distribution/EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE

# List active protections
aws shield list-protections \
  --query 'Protections[*].[Name,ResourceArn]' \
  --output table

# View attack events (last 30 days)
aws shield describe-attacks \
  --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-01-31T23:59:59Z \
  --query 'AttackSummaries[*].[AttackId,StartTime,AttackVectors[0].VectorType]'
Amazon Route 53: DNS at the Edge

Route 53 resolves DNS queries from the same anycast fleet as CloudFront. The nameserver IPs for every Route 53 hosted zone are advertised from all PoPs simultaneously. A DNS resolver anywhere in the world sends its query to the nearest PoP and gets a response in under 10 ms — fast enough that DNS is rarely the bottleneck in page load time.
Returns a fixed value; no health checks
Distributes traffic by weight ratio across records
Routes to the AWS Region with lowest measured latency from the user's PoP
Routes by distance, with bias controls
Returns up to 8 healthy records at random; client chooses
AWS WAF: Web Application Firewall at the Edge

evaluates a Web ACL against every HTTP/HTTPS request that CloudFront or Global Accelerator forwards. Rule evaluation happens at the PoP — a blocked request is dropped in milliseconds, before any data reaches the origin. This means WAF protection has near-zero added latency for allowed traffic: inspection adds about 1 ms per request.
: AWS-maintained rulesets for OWASP Top 10, known bad IPs, SQL injection, XSS. Enabled with one API call, updated automatically.
: Match on IP address, country, URI, HTTP headers, query strings, body. Combine conditions with AND/OR/NOT.
: Automatically block IPs that exceed N requests per 5 minutes. Effective against credential stuffing and scraping.
: Identify and manage bots using browser fingerprinting, CAPTCHA challenges, and known bot signatures.
scope (global, evaluated at every PoP) or
scope (evaluated at the ALB or API Gateway in a specific Region). Always use CLOUDFRONT scope for publicly-facing distributions.
is fundamentally different from CloudFront: it is a

, not a CDN. It provides two static Anycast IP addresses that route TCP or UDP traffic to the closest healthy endpoint across Regions using the AWS private backbone — bypassing congested public internet paths for the cross-region leg.
Yes — SSD cache at every PoP
Non-HTTP protocols (gaming, VoIP, IoT), HTTP APIs needing static IPs
Web content, API responses, video streaming
Per-endpoint health checks; traffic shifts in <30s

# Create a Global Accelerator with two static Anycast IPs
aws globalaccelerator create-accelerator \
  --name my-global-app \
  --ip-address-type IPV4 \
  --enabled \
  --region us-east-1

# Add a listener for TCP port 443
ACCEL_ARN=$(aws globalaccelerator list-accelerators \
  --query 'Accelerators[?Name==`my-global-app`].AcceleratorArn' \
  --output text --region us-east-1)

aws globalaccelerator create-listener \
  --accelerator-arn $ACCEL_ARN \
  --protocol TCP \
  --port-ranges '[{"FromPort": 443, "ToPort": 443}]' \
  --region us-east-1

# Add endpoint groups (one per Region for multi-region active-active)
LISTENER_ARN=$(aws globalaccelerator list-listeners \
  --accelerator-arn $ACCEL_ARN \
  --query 'Listeners[0].ListenerArn' --output text --region us-east-1)

aws globalaccelerator create-endpoint-group \
  --listener-arn $LISTENER_ARN \
  --endpoint-group-region us-east-1 \
  --endpoints '[{"EndpointId": "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/my-alb/abc123", "Weight": 128}]' \
  --region us-east-1

CloudFront handles Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS caching and is the most feature-rich edge service. It not only caches content but also runs JavaScript (CloudFront Functions) or full Node.js/Python runtimes (Lambda@Edge) at every PoP.
Each CloudFront distribution can have multiple
— path-pattern rules that determine how different URL prefixes are cached, compressed, forwarded, and secured.

# Check effective cache policy on a distribution
aws cloudfront get-distribution-config \
  --id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
  --query 'DistributionConfig.CacheBehaviors.Items[*].{Path:PathPattern,Policy:CachePolicyId}'

# Create a custom cache policy — normalize Accept-Encoding header, ignore cookies
aws cloudfront create-cache-policy --cache-policy-config '{
  "Name": "OptimizedStaticAssets",
  "DefaultTTL": 86400,
  "MaxTTL": 31536000,
  "MinTTL": 0,
  "ParametersInCacheKeyAndForwardedToOrigin": {
    "EnableAcceptEncodingGzip": true,
    "EnableAcceptEncodingBrotli": true,
    "HeadersConfig": {"HeaderBehavior": "none"},
    "CookiesConfig": {"CookieBehavior": "none"},
    "QueryStringsConfig": {"QueryStringBehavior": "none"}
  }
}'

# Associate a Lambda@Edge function at origin-request event
# (must be created in us-east-1 for all Lambda@Edge)

# Example: origin-request handler that adds auth header before forwarding to origin
cat > /tmp/edge-auth.py << 'EOF'
import json, base64, os

def handler(event, context):
    request = event['Records'][0]['cf']['request']
    # Inject an internal service auth header
    request['headers']['x-internal-auth'] = [{
        'key': 'X-Internal-Auth',
        'value': os.environ['INTERNAL_TOKEN']
    }]
    return request
EOF

# Deploy as Lambda function in us-east-1, then associate with distribution
aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
  --distribution-config file://dist-with-edge.json

These five services are not mutually exclusive — they are commonly deployed together as a defense-in-depth edge stack. A typical production architecture for a public web application uses all five, each handling a distinct concern.
resolves DNS to a CloudFront distribution (CNAME alias) using latency routing to pick the best Region.
absorbs volumetric DDoS at Layer 3/4 as packets arrive at the PoP — no configuration required.
evaluates the Web ACL at Layer 7: drops known-bad IPs, SQL injection payloads, and excessive request rates.
run sub-millisecond viewer-request logic: URL normalization, security headers, A/B test cookie assignment.
serves the response from PoP SSD if the cache key matches — origin not involved.
backbone routing carries the miss request to the closest healthy origin Region.
at origin-request can authenticate, route, or rewrite the request before it reaches the origin ALB.
Cache HIT: ~0 ms; Lambda cold start: ~50 ms
Select based on protocol, whether caching is needed, and whether you need static IPs.
Serve static files / images / video from cache
Global Accelerator or CloudFront with no-cache policy
Block SQL injection, XSS, bots at edge
Route DNS by region / latency / health
Rewrite URLs, inject headers, A/B logic at edge
Static Anycast IPs that never change

# CloudFront: cache hit rate over the last hour
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/CloudFront \
  --metric-name CacheHitRate \
  --dimensions Name=DistributionId,Value=EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
               Name=Region,Value=Global \
  --period 3600 --statistics Average \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '1h ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

# WAF: count blocked requests by rule
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/WAFV2 \
  --metric-name BlockedRequests \
  --dimensions Name=WebACL,Value=MyWebACL \
               Name=Region,Value=us-east-1 \
               Name=Rule,Value=AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet \
  --period 300 --statistics Sum \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '1h ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

# Global Accelerator: endpoint health across Regions
aws globalaccelerator describe-accelerator \
  --accelerator-arn $ACCEL_ARN \
  --query 'Accelerator.{Status:Status,IpSets:IpSets[*].IpAddresses}' \
  --region us-east-1
Data out volume and request count
CloudFront and WAF charges are additive — deploying WAF on CloudFront bills both services.
benefit can refund EC2/data-transfer overcharges during a DDoS event — important for protecting against billing spikes.
Global Accelerator adds a premium over CloudFront for pure HTTP workloads; justify it by the static-IP requirement or non-HTTP protocol need.
Route 53 queries to CloudFront distributions using alias records are free — alias resolution is not billed.
Infrastructure-as-code for the full edge stack — Route 53, CloudFront with WAF, and Global Accelerator — keeps configurations reproducible and reviewable.
S3 bucket hosts HTML/JS/CSS; CloudFront caches globally; WAF blocks bots
Route 53 (latency) + CloudFront + ALB in 2+ Regions + Global Accelerator
Route 53 picks nearest Region; CloudFront caches GET responses; GA accelerates uncached requests
CloudFront + S3 origin + Shield Advanced + MediaPackage
CloudFront serves HLS/DASH segments from cache; Shield protects against streaming floods
Lambda@Edge validates JWT on every request before forwarding to origin
Shield Advanced + WAF + Global Accelerator + ALB
GA provides static IPs for allowlisting; Shield + WAF absorb and filter attack traffic

Adding HTTP security headers (Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options) at the PoP via CloudFront Functions is more efficient than setting them at the origin — the function runs in under 1 ms and applies to every cached and non-cached response.

Deploy security headers at CloudFront rather than origin so they apply even when the response is served from cache — origin-set headers on a cached response are not re-injected on subsequent cache hits.
After deploying edge services, verify each layer independently before assuming the stack is correct.

# 1. Confirm DNS resolves correctly and uses the right PoP
dig +short www.example.com
nslookup -type=A www.example.com 8.8.8.8

# 2. Check CloudFront cache behavior and PoP identity
curl -sI https://www.example.com | grep -iE 'x-cache|x-amz-cf-pop|age|server'

# 3. Verify WAF is evaluating requests (check sampled requests)
aws wafv2 get-sampled-requests \
  --web-acl-arn arn:aws:wafv2:us-east-1:123456789012:global/webacl/edge-waf/abc123 \
  --rule-metric-name CommonRules \
  --scope CLOUDFRONT \
  --time-window 'StartTime=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z,EndTime=2024-01-01T01:00:00Z' \
  --max-items 5 \
  --query 'SampledRequests[*].[Request.URI,Response.ResponseCode,Action]'

# 4. Test WAF blocking by sending a SQL injection probe (in a test environment only)
curl -sv 'https://www.example.com/?id=1;DROP TABLE users' 2>&1 | grep '< HTTP'
# Expected: HTTP/2 403 if WAF CommonRuleSet is active

# 5. Verify TLS negotiates TLS 1.2+
openssl s_client -connect www.example.com:443 -servername www.example.com 2>/dev/null \
  | grep -E 'Protocol|Cipher'
is always-on, free, and automatic — every CloudFront and Route 53 deployment gets L3/L4 DDoS protection with no configuration.
uses the same anycast PoP fleet as CloudFront; routing policies (latency, geo, failover) make DNS an active traffic management tool, not just name resolution.
runs at the PoP before cache lookup, adding ~1 ms inspection overhead while blocking known exploits and rate-abusing IPs before they reach your origin.
solves the static-IP and non-HTTP-protocol gaps that CloudFront cannot fill — it routes TCP/UDP over the AWS backbone for consistent cross-region latency.
is the workhorse: caching, TLS offload, and edge compute (CloudFront Functions + Lambda@Edge) in one service. When in doubt, CloudFront is the starting point.
Layering all five services (Shield → Route 53 → WAF → CloudFront → Global Accelerator for miss routing) creates a complete edge stack covering DDoS, DNS, security, caching, and acceleration.
